risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Infants were randomized to receive one of two liquid supplements to be taken daily until they reached 18 months of age. Randomization was in blocks of two stratified by infant gender." Comment: This describes a random allocation process with random permuted blocks/stratification, which is sufficient to indicate a proper random sequence was used; no non-random or predictable rule is described.

_evidence.method_quote: Infants were randomized to receive one of two liquid supplements to be taken daily until they reached 18 months of age. Randomization was in blocks of two stratified by infant gender.
_evidence.method_summary: Randomized in blocks of two, stratified by infant gender.
context_clues: Randomization was in blocks of two stratified by infant gender

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable. The bottles had spoons metered to deliver 2.5 mL of supplement. A set of bottles were coded with each subject’s unique study identification number to facilitate distribution. The allocation list was held by the pharmaceutical company and the Director of the Instituto de Investigacion Nutricional (IIN) until the end of the study." Comment: The allocation list was not accessible to recruiters and study product was dispensed using coded, identical bottles, which is an explicit mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable. The bottles had spoons metered to deliver 2.5 mL of supplement. A set of bottles were coded with each subject’s unique study identification number to facilitate distribution. The allocation list was held by the pharmaceutical company and the Director of the Instituto de Investigacion Nutricional (IIN) until the end of the study.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable." Comment: This indicates participants and personnel were provided with visually identical coded supplements, and the study reports that the allocation list was held separately until the end of the study, making successful blinding of participants/personnel likely. The main outcomes were largely objective biochemical measures (hemoglobin, ferritin, copper, zinc, urinary zinc), so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have materially affected the dominant outcome profile.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable.
_evidence.blinding_method: indistinguishable liquid supplements in coded bottles

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable." Comment: This shows blinding of the supplements' appearance, but the paper does not explicitly state that outcome assessors were blinded. The outcomes include mostly objective laboratory measures (hemoglobin, ferritin, plasma copper, plasma zinc, urinary zinc), which are unlikely to be influenced by assessor knowledge, but the study also reports patient-reported outcomes such as "illness since the last visit" and "supplement administration" in the extracted evidence. Because explicit outcome-assessor blinding is not stated and the evidence for the patient-reported outcomes' assessment blinding is unclear, the domain cannot be rated Low; however, there is also no positive evidence that the assessor was unblinded, so High is not justified.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "As shown, there were 42 (13%) infants who left the study, leaving 108 in the iron + copper group and 101 in the iron + copper + zinc group." Comment: The analysed outcome data show per-arm post-randomization loss of 14/122 (11.5%) vs 28/129 (21.7%), a differential loss of about 11.2 percentage points, which exceeds the High-risk threshold. The paper also reports that those lost to follow up were more likely to be allocated to the zinc group and that more children left the study in the zinc group due to lack of time or dislike of the supplement, indicating attrition plausibly related to the intervention/outcome process. Although some biochemical subsamples were additionally restricted by funds/sample volume, the main attrition on the analysed study outcomes is sufficient to judge High risk.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “there were 42 (13%) infants who left the study, leaving 108 in the iron + copper group”
“there were 42 (13%) infants who left the study, leaving 108 in the iron + copper group and 101 in the iron + copper + zinc group”

_evidence.notes: Attrition numbers are from the overall study flow; the biochemical outcomes also note that "It was not possible to conduct full laboratory analyses for iron, copper and zinc status on all infants" and that sample selection for analysis was based on "funds, study completion and sample volume, with no consideration of treatment allocation or compliance."
